page: Vultr vhp-4c-12gb-amd is a virtual machine instance belonging to the High Performance family, featuring 4 shared vCPUs mapped to 4 physical cores of an AMD EPYC-Rome processor. This x86_64 architecture operates at a clock speed of 2.0 GHz, supported by 16 MB of L3 cache, 12.0 GB of system memory providing 3.0 GB per core, and 260 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. The instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address and does not feature a GPU.

Benchmark data reveals a mixed performance profile. The instance demonstrates strong single-core CPU performance in stress-ng tests, placing in the top 25%. However, multi-core CPU performance and memory bandwidth for cached small blocks fall into the average tier. Uncached large-block memory read and write speeds, along with Passmark CPU and memory scores, are positioned in the weak to poor categories. Data decompression and database operations also score in the bottom 10% to 25%, indicating potential bottlenecks during heavy memory-bound or complex database tasks.

Given its shared CPU allocation, this instance offers a cost-efficient entry point for workloads that require moderate compute resources and local NVMe storage without the premium of dedicated cores. The primary tradeoff is the shared CPU model and lower memory bandwidth, which limits its suitability for high-throughput database operations or intensive data processing. It is best suited for general-purpose applications, static web serving, light background tasks, and development environments where sustained maximum CPU performance is not critical.
description: Vultr vhp-4c-12gb-amd is a virtual machine instance in the High Performance family, configured with 4 shared AMD EPYC-Rome vCPUs running at 2.0 GHz, 12.0 GB of system memory, and 260 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Operating on the x86_64 architecture, this instance lacks a GPU but includes one public IPv4 address. Performance benchmarks show strong single-core CPU capabilities in stress-ng tests, though multi-core performance and memory bandwidth for large uncached blocks fall into the average and weak categories, respectively. Passmark database operations and bzip3 decompression benchmarks rank in the lower percentiles, highlighting performance tradeoffs under heavy memory or data processing loads. This shared-CPU instance provides a cost-effective option for workloads such as static web serving, development environments, and general-purpose applications that do not require dedicated compute resources.
